title: Labs
shortDescription: A self-maintaining project showcase with a creative animation layer built from scratch — terminal boot sequence, canvas dot-field background, datamosh view transitions, ghost-type headline decryption, and proximity-reactive cards. Speculative Interface design system, embedded Digital Twin, nightly auto-sync.
detailedDescription:
Labs v2 opens with a System Boot sequence: a full-viewport terminal overlay types out monospace log lines (INIT, LOAD, SCAN, SYNC, BOOT) with chartreuse status flashes, then wipes upward to reveal the site. The overlay is session-gated via `sessionStorage` — first visit only — with a `REPLAY_BOOT_SEQUENCE` easter egg wired to a footer trigger. Behind every page, a Signal Field canvas renders a responsive dot grid across the viewport: on desktop, dots near the cursor brighten, shift toward chartreuse, and pull slightly toward the pointer; on mobile, an ambient sine wave ripples through the field on a continuous `requestAnimationFrame` loop.

Headlines use Ghost Type — a `requestAnimationFrame`-driven scramble that cycles through Unicode glyphs and resolves characters left-to-right like a decryption sequence. It fires on the hero headline after boot completes and on project titles when entering detail view. Navigating between grid and detail views triggers Datamosh: horizontal slice displacement with per-slice RGB channel separation and a chartreuse scan-line sweep. Full mode (grid-to-detail, 350ms) runs heavier displacement; mild mode (detail-to-grid, 300ms) pulls back. The grid itself is proximity-aware: Proximity Pulse tilts cards up to 5 degrees toward the cursor, lifts the nearest card with a depth shadow, compresses adjacent cards, and renders a directional chartreuse radial glow tracking cursor position. Desktop only — mobile skips the effect cleanly.

Underneath the animation layer, the grid uses a `buildGridItems` algorithm tracking column position across a 3-column layout: highlighted projects span two columns, filler cards (glitch-style corrupted hex blocks or system-diagnostic telemetry with uptime counter and scrolling hex dump) fill gaps and pad rows. The design system is Speculative Interface: pale blue-grey (`#F0F2F5`) base, chartreuse (`#C8FF00`) accent, Space Grotesk typography, light-mode only, all tokens scoped under `html.v2` so v1 tokens remain intact. Every screen was prototyped in Stitch MCP before code.

Originally a dark glassmorphic interface with electric blue accent and Framer Motion scroll-ins — that shipped as v1. The self-maintaining layer carries forward: a nightly GitHub Actions workflow calls the GitHub API for each showcased project and auto-commits updated `version` and `lastUpdated` values to main. The Digital Twin chat engine is embedded with scale-and-fade open/close animation, ghost-type header on each open, and a chartreuse glow pulse on the toggle button. Redis-backed session persistence and a Telegram bot share conversation history via OTP pairing.
keyLearnings:
Ghost Type runs entirely on `requestAnimationFrame` with a per-character reveal index — no `setTimeout` chains, no CSS animations. Each frame picks a random Unicode glyph for every unrevealed character and advances the resolve cursor by one position. The tradeoff: frame rate directly controls scramble speed, so on low-refresh displays the effect looks noticeably faster. Datamosh timing was the hardest animation to land — the 350ms full-mode and 300ms mild-mode durations were arrived at by testing on 60Hz and 120Hz displays, because the slice displacement and RGB channel separation read as broken rendering if they linger even 50ms too long.

Signal Field renders ~2,400 dots on a full-viewport canvas at 60fps. The performance constraint: every dot checks cursor distance each frame, so the proximity calculation uses squared distance (skipping `Math.sqrt`) and early-exits dots beyond the influence radius. On mobile, the sine-wave ripple replaces cursor tracking entirely — no pointer events, just a time-based phase offset per dot. The System Boot overlay is session-gated via `sessionStorage` rather than `localStorage`, a deliberate choice so clearing the tab (not just the session) replays the sequence — and the footer easter egg writes a flag to `sessionStorage` to force a replay on next navigation.

The nightly GitHub Actions workflow auto-commits `version` and `lastUpdated` back to `main` using `[skip ci]` to prevent feedback loops. The v2 redesign validated prototyping in Stitch MCP before code — the filler card insertion logic and card hover treatment were resolved at the comp stage. Scoping v2 tokens under `html.v2` while leaving v1 on `:root` avoided specificity conflicts, at the cost of non-portable `var(--v2-*)` references in every v2 component.

title: Diana's Digital Twin
shortDescription: A conversational agent that answers questions about Diana's work, background, and approach — in her voice, across web and Telegram, drawing from tiered context rather than fine-tuning. Built with FastAPI, OpenAI, and Redis.
detailedDescription:
Visitors can ask questions about Diana's work, background, and professional decisions — the agent responds in her voice, drawing from structured context layers: a persistent base context covering her career and approach, and keyword-triggered on-demand context (project notes, specific case details) loaded only when relevant. Conversation history persists via Redis, so sessions continue across page loads. The same conversation thread is accessible on Telegram via an OTP pairing flow: generate a code in the web widget, enter it in Telegram, and both interfaces share a single session.

The Twin engine is embedded directly into Labs as a floating chat widget — SSE-streamed responses with the same conversation backend, restyled to match the v2 Speculative Interface. It also powers the chat on Diana's portfolio site, so one engine serves both frontends without duplication. Built with FastAPI and OpenAI GPT, with a modular context pipeline designed to add new knowledge sources without changing the core inference loop.
keyLearnings: Tiered context injection — a small always-on base context plus keyword-triggered on-demand blocks — reduced token usage by ~57% compared to injecting the full knowledge base on every request. The tradeoff: keyword matching must be precise enough to trigger the right context without false negatives, which required building a separate trigger-tuning layer before the quality bar was stable.

title: CV/JD Matcher & Application Tool
shortDescription: Reso scores a CV against a job description, surfaces gaps with specific improvement suggestions, and tracks the full application pipeline — matching analysis, interview prep packs, and logged sessions with AI-generated notes. Built with Next.js, OpenAI, and PostgreSQL.
detailedDescription: Upload a CV and paste a job description into Reso: the system scores the match, surfaces strengths and gaps, and generates a tailored CV variant and interview prep pack for that specific role. The job tracker maintains a full application pipeline — each application stores its AI analysis, logged prep sessions with GPT-generated notes, and a dashboard view of progress across roles. Navigation between applications uses `sessionStorage` rather than URL params, a deliberate privacy decision that keeps job UUIDs out of browser history and requires SSR guard handling on the Next.js side. Built with Next.js, OpenAI, PostgreSQL, and NextAuth for session management.
keyLearnings: The `sessionStorage` navigation pattern (passing job UUIDs via `sessionStorage` rather than URL params) was a deliberate privacy and UX decision that required careful `SSR` guard handling in Next.js.

title: AI Chat Scheduler
shortDescription: A conversational AI assistant that helps event attendees discover sessions and build conflict-free personal schedules for multi-day events. Built with the Vercel AI SDK and OpenAI.
detailedDescription: Attendees type questions into the chat — 'show me workshops on Thursday afternoon' or 'build me a schedule that avoids the keynote clashes' — and the assistant returns recommendations and generates a downloadable itinerary. The scheduling logic is driven by structured markers embedded in the AI's streamed output: when the model determines a schedule is ready, it emits a `[GENERATE_SCHEDULE]` token that the client intercepts to trigger the PDF-generation flow, keeping UI state changes decoupled from prompt engineering. There is no form-based configuration for organisers; the event data is loaded as structured context at session start. Built with Vercel AI SDK, OpenAI GPT, Next.js 16, and TypeScript 5.
keyLearnings: AI-emitted markers (`[GENERATE_SCHEDULE]`, `schedule_download` JSON blocks) trigger client-side behaviours from within streamed responses — bridging LLM output and UI state without post-processing the full response. The constraint: markers must appear at predictable positions in the stream; if the model embeds them mid-sentence or omits them under ambiguous prompts, the UI silently skips the action.

title: GEO Audit
shortDescription: A tool that tests any webpage's visibility to AI search engines — Perplexity, Gemini, ChatGPT. Returns a scored report with specific, actionable improvements. Built with React 19, Express.js, Gemini, and OpenAI.
detailedDescription: Submit a URL and GEO Audit fetches the page content, runs it through scoring dimensions — fact density, entity salience, extractability, and platform-specific citation patterns — and returns a prioritised report. The free tier covers the core dimensions; the advanced tier adds competitor gap analysis and platform-specific recommendations. Results render as charts with a PDF export. The frontend and backend are fully decoupled: React 19 on Vite communicates with an Express API via environment-configured endpoints, with `VITE_*` vars baked into the browser bundle at build time and all API keys restricted to the server layer. Built with React 19, Vite, Express, Gemini, OpenAI, PostgreSQL, and deployed on Railway.
keyLearnings: `VITE_*` environment variables are bundled into the client at build time and visible in the browser — so any variable the frontend reads is effectively public. Keeping the two-tier feature logic on the server (Express decides what the free tier returns) rather than in the frontend bundle was not optional: feature flags in the client are easily bypassed.

title: Slack ↔ Claude CLI Bridge
shortDescription: An internal Slack bot that routes direct messages and @mentions to Claude — via the local CLI or the Anthropic API — with GPT-powered Gmail digest and team inbox commands. Built with Node.js, Slack Bolt SDK, and the Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gmail APIs.
detailedDescription: Message Claude directly inside Slack without leaving your workspace: the bot responds to DMs and @mentions, routing each request to Claude in either CLI mode (shelling out to the local `claude` binary, no API key required) or API mode (calling the Anthropic API directly), switchable via a single environment variable. Two slash commands extend it further: `/digest` fetches unread Gmail for up to three configured accounts, classifies messages by priority using GPT-5.2, and returns a formatted summary; `/owner-inbox` scans a designated folder of files your team has shared with you and suggests prioritised actions. Two Slack transport options — Socket Mode for local development without a tunnel, HTTP mode for cloud or ngrok deployments — mean the same codebase runs from a laptop or a Railway instance. Releases are automated via semantic-release; merging to `main` bumps the version and publishes a changelog automatically.
keyLearnings: Supporting two Claude backends (CLI subprocess vs. direct API call) behind a single env-var switch exposed a hard constraint: CLI mode works only when the `claude` binary is authenticated on the local machine, so cloud deployment silently requires `USE_CLI=false` — a dependency that must be documented, not assumed.

title: Diana Ismail — Portfolio
shortDescription: Diana's portfolio — built from scratch to the same standard she applies to client work. AI-powered project summaries, an embedded digital twin, and a CMS the Owner can update without touching code. Not a template. Not outsourced. Built.
detailedDescription: A ground-up migration from WordPress to Next.js 16, designed and built by Diana's AI team. Every project page includes an AI Context Panel — a collapsible sidebar on desktop, inline accordion on mobile — that streams a neutral, AI-generated summary of the work on demand. The Digital Twin chat engine is embedded site-wide: a floating widget on every page, and the primary interface on the Ask Diana page. Conversation history syncs across both via Redis, so a visitor can start in the widget and continue on the full page without losing context. The site uses locally hosted fonts to avoid build-time network dependencies, WordPress URL redirects to preserve old bookmarks, and WCAG AA-compliant theming in both light and dark mode. All content is managed through Sanity Studio — the Owner updates copy, projects, and metadata without code changes. Deployed on Railway with semantic-release for automated versioning.
keyLearnings: Turbopack on Railway cannot reach Google Fonts during production builds — switching to local woff2 files via `next/font/local` eliminated the network dependency entirely. Also: the parent nav's `backdrop-blur` creates a CSS stacking context that traps `position: fixed` children — a portal to `document.body` was the clean escape.
